name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَمِنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ مَن يُنكِرُ بَعْضَهُۥ ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱللَّهَ وَلَآ أُشْرِكَ بِهِۦٓ ۚ إِلَيْهِ أَدْعُوا۟ وَإِلَيْهِ مَـَٔابِ (٣٦)
```

```english
Those whom We have given the Book rejoice in what has been sent down to you. Some of the factions deny a part of it. Say, ‘Indeed I have been commanded to worship Allah and not to ascribe any partner to Him. To Him do I summon [all mankind] and to Him will be my return.’ (36)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا عَرَبِيًّا ۚ وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم بَعْدَ مَا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ مَا لَكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا وَاقٍ (٣٧)
```

```english
Thus We have sent it down as a dispensation in Arabic; and should you follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you, you shall have against Allah neither any guardian nor defender. (37)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلًا مِّن قَبْلِكَ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا وَذُرِّيَّةً ۚ وَمَا كَانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتَابٌ (٣٨)
```

```english
Certainly We have sent apostles before you, and We appointed for them wives and descendants; and an apostle may not bring a sign except by Allah’s leave. There is a written [ordinance] for every term: (38)
```

```arabic
يَمْحُوا۟ ٱللَّهُ مَا يَشَآءُ وَيُثْبِتُ ۖ وَعِندَهُۥٓ أُمُّ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (٣٩)
```

```english
Allah effaces and confirms whatever He wishes and with Him is the Mother Book. (39)
```

```arabic
وَإِن مَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ وَعَلَيْنَا ٱلْحِسَابُ (٤٠)
```

```english
Whether We show you a part of what We promise them, or take you away [before that], your duty is only to communicate, and it is for Us to do the reckoning. (40)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا نَأْتِى ٱلْأَرْضَ نَنقُصُهَا مِنْ أَطْرَافِهَا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ لَا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٤١)
```

```english
Have they not seen how We visit the land diminishing it at its edges? Allah judges, and there is none who may repeal His judgement, and He is swift at reckoning. (41)
```

```arabic
وَقَدْ مَكَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْمَكْرُ جَمِيعًا ۖ يَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ۗ وَسَيَعْلَمُ ٱلْكُفَّـٰرُ لِمَنْ عُقْبَى ٱلدَّارِ (٤٢)
```

```english
Those who were before them [also] schemed; yet all devising belongs to Allah. He knows what every soul earns. Soon the faithless will know in whose favour the outcome of that abode will be. (42)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a continuation of the previous verses and a response to their saying: **لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ** — "Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?" (13:27).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ** — **Those to whom We have given the Book rejoice in what has been sent down to you** (13:36) until the end of the verse. It appears that "those to whom the Book was given" refers to the Jews and the Christians, or perhaps them along with the Magians (المجوس), as this is the customary usage in the Quranic idiom. This Surah is Meccan, and history confirms that the Jews did not initially oppose the Arabian Prophethood at the beginning of the Mission (البعثة) and prior to it with the same level of animosity that the events following the Migration (الهجرة) later provoked. Indeed, a group of them entered Islam at the beginning of the Migration and bore witness to the Prophethood of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the fact that he was foretold in their books, as the Exalted says: **وَشَهدَ شاهِدٌ مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ عَلى مِثْلِهِ فَآمَنَ وَاسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ** — **...and a witness from the Children of Israel has testified to its like and believed, while you were regained with pride?** (46:10).

Furthermore, there were among the Christians at that time a people upon the truth who did not oppose the call of Islam, such as the group of Christians from Abyssinia, according to what has been narrated regarding the story of the migration to Abyssinia, and others besides them. Regarding such people, the Exalted has said: **الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ هُمْ بِهِ يُؤْمِنُونَ** — **Those to whom We gave the Book before it are the ones who believe in it** (28:52), and He said: **وَمِنْ قَوْمِ مُوسى أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ** — **Among the people of Moses is a nation who guide [others] by the truth and do justice thereby** (7:159). Similarly, the Magians were awaiting deliverance through the appearance of a savior who would spread truth and justice, and they did not oppose the truth with the same vehemence as the polytheists (المشركون).

Thus, it appears that they are the ones intended by the verse, especially the righteous among the Christians—those who maintained that the Messiah was a human messenger, such as the Negus (النجاشي) and his companions. This is supported by the conclusion of the verse: **قُلْ إِنَّما أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ اللهَ وَلا أُشْرِكَ بِهِ إِلَيْهِ أَدْعُوا** — **Say, ‘I have been commanded only to worship Allah and not to ascribe any partner to Him. To Him do I invite [all mankind]’** (13:36), for this address is most appropriate when directed toward the Christians.

His saying: **وَمِنَ الْأَحْزابِ مَنْ يُنْكِرُ بَعْضَهُ** — **...and of the factions there are those who deny part of it** (13:36). The definite article (اللام) here is for specific reference (للعهد), meaning: and among the factions of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) are those who deny part of what has been sent down to you—namely, that which indicates monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and the negation of the Trinity, as well as other matters that contradict the distorted knowledge and laws held by the People of the Book.

His saying: **قُلْ إِنَّما أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ اللهَ وَلا أُشْرِكَ بِهِ** — **Say, ‘I have been commanded only to worship Allah and not to ascribe any partner to Him’** (13:36) is evidence that the "part" they deny pertains to the oneness of worship or obedience. Allah commanded him to address them by seeking agreement upon this, saying: **قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ تَعالَوْا إِلى كَلِمَةٍ سَواءٍ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللهَ وَلا نُشْرِكَ بِهِ شَيْئاً وَلا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** — **Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you: that we will worship no one but Allah, that we will not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us will take others as lords besides Allah.’** (3:64).

Then He completed the statement by saying: **إِلَيْهِ أَدْعُوا وَإِلَيْهِ مَآبِ** — **...To Him do I invite [all mankind] and to Him is my return** (13:36). Thus, the beginning of the statement expressed his objective for himself and others, while its end expressed his conduct (سيرته). That is: I have been commanded to worship Allah alone in my actions and my call, and upon that I proceed among the people; I do not invite except to Him, and I do not return in any of my affairs except to Him. Thus, the conclusion of the verse carries the same meaning as His saying: **قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللهِ عَلى بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي وَسُبْحانَ اللهِ وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** — **Say, ‘This is my way. I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me. Immaculate is Allah, and I am not one of the polytheists.’** (12:108).

It is also possible that the intended meaning of **وَإِلَيْهِ مَآبِ** is the Resurrection (المعاد), in which case it serves as a justification (التعليل), meaning: I invite to Him alone because my return is to Him alone.

Some commentators have interpreted "the Book" (الكتاب) in the verse as the Quran, "those to whom the Book was given" as the companions of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and "the factions" (الأحزاب) as the desert Arabs (الأعراب) who leagued against the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and plotted against him from among the Quraysh and other tribes.

However, this is contrary to the customary Quranic usage of the phrase **الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ** (those to whom the Book was given). Furthermore, such an interpretation would result in the verse containing a redundant meaning.

Others have mentioned that the intended meaning refers specifically to the Jews, the Book being the Torah, and the denial by some of their factions of "part" of the Quran refers to that which does not agree with their distorted laws. This is despite the fact that all of them deny whatever does not agree with what they have, doing so without joy, while the rest were both rejoicing and denying. They have engaged in lengthy discussions regarding this.

It is narrated from some that the relative pronoun (الموصول) refers to the Muslims in general, and "the factions" refers to the Jews, Christians, and Magians. From others, it is narrated that the implied meaning of **وَإِلَيْهِ مَآبِ** is "to Him is my return and your return." These are opinions for which there is no evidence in the wording, and there is no benefit in prolonging the discussion about them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكَذلِكَ أَنْزَلْناهُ حُكْماً عَرَبِيًّا وَلَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتَ أَهْواءَهُمْ بَعْدَ ما جاءَكَ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ ما لَكَ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا واقٍ** — **Thus have We sent it down as a dispensation in Arabic. And should you follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you, you shall have against Allah no guardian or defender.** (13:37). The demonstration in His saying **كَذلِكَ** (Thus) refers to the Book mentioned in the previous verse, which is the genus of the Book revealed to the past prophets, such as the Torah and the Gospel.

The intended meaning of "dispensation" (الحُكم) is the decree (القضاء) and the firm resolve (العزيمة), for that is the function of the Book sent down from heaven containing the Divine Law (الشريعة), as He said: **وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ** — **...and He sent down with them the Book with the truth to judge between the people concerning that about which they differed.** (2:213). Thus, the Book is a Divine decree (حكم إلهي) in one respect and a judge (حاكم) among the people in another. This is the intended meaning of *al-hukm*, not "wisdom" (الحكمة) as has been suggested.

His saying **عَرَبِيًّا** (in Arabic) is an attribute of the "dispensation" (حكم) and an indication that the Book is in the Arabic tongue, which was his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله tongue, following the way of Allah (سنة الله) that has passed among His servants. The Exalted said: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسانِ قَوْمِهِ** — **We did not send any apostle except with the language of his people...** (14:4). This—as is not hidden—is evidence that those mentioned in the previous verse are the Jews and Christians, and that these verses address their state, just as the verses preceding them addressed the state of the polytheists.

Based on this, the intended meaning of His saying **وَلَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتَ أَهْواءَهُمْ** (And should you follow their desires) etc., is a prohibition against following the desires of the People of the Book. Much regarding this has been mentioned in the Quran. The core of it was that they used to propose to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله a sign other than the Quran, just as the polytheists proposed, and they hoped he would follow them in the laws they possessed due to their deeming abrogation (النسخ) — *the replacement of a previous divine ruling with a new one* — in laws to be impossible. These two matters, especially the first, are the primary subjects addressed by these verses.

The meaning is: Just as We sent down to those who were given the Book their book, We have sent down this Quran to you in your tongue, containing a decree or acting as a judge among the people. And should you follow the desires of the People of the Book—such that you wish for a sign other than the Quran to be sent down to you as they propose, or you compromise with them and incline toward following some of the abrogated or distorted laws they possess—We would seize you with punishment, and you would have no guardian (ولي) to take charge of your affair besides Allah, nor any defender (واق) to protect you from Him. Thus, the address is to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and he is the one intended by it, not the community (الأمة) as some have mentioned.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلاً مِنْ قَبْلِكَ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُمْ أَزْواجاً وَذُرِّيَّةً وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتابٌ** — **Certainly We have sent apostles before you, and We appointed for them wives and descendants; and an apostle may not bring a sign except by Allah’s leave. For every term there is a writing.** (13:38). When He prohibited the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله from following their desires regarding the sign they proposed he bring other than the Quran, He reminded him of the reality of the situation to despair him of any hope in that regard and to resolve him to rely upon Allah and return all matters to Him.

This reality is that the established way of Allah (سنة الله) regarding the messengers is that they are human beings, proceeding according to the customary way familiar among people, without exceeding it to possess anything belonging to the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge*. They do not possess an unseen power that can effect whatever it wishes, nor are they capable of everything they desire or is desired of them, such that they could bring any sign they wished, except if Allah grants them permission. The messenger, being a human like others, has no authority in the matter; rather, all authority belongs to Allah.

He is the One who sends down the sign if He wills. However, He, the Glorified, only sends down signs when Divine wisdom (الحكمة الإلهية) necessitates it. Times are not identical or equal in their rulings and interests; otherwise, wisdom would be nullified and the order of creation would be disrupted. Rather, for every time there is a wisdom that suits it and a ruling that suits it; thus, for every time there is a sign specific to it.

This is what the verse indicates. His saying: **وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلاً مِنْ قَبْلِكَ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُمْ أَزْواجاً وَذُرِّيَّةً** is an indication of the established way regarding the messengers in their ordinary humanity. His saying: **وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** is an indication of their lack of independent unseen power to do whatever they wish, unless Divine permission (الإذن الإلهي) supports them.

His saying: **لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ** — meaning a limited time — **كِتابٌ** — meaning a decreed and written ruling (حكم مقضي مكتوب) specific to it. This is an indication of what is alluded to by the exception of permission and the way of Allah acting within it. The implied meaning is: Allah, the Glorified, is the One who sends down what He wills and permits what He wills, but He does not send down nor permit every sign at every time, for every time has a writing (كتاب) He has written, and nothing occurs within it except what is in it.

From what has preceded, it becomes clear that the view mentioned by some—that the phrase **لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتابٌ** is a case of inversion (القلب) and its original form is "for every writing there is a term" (لكل كتاب أجل), meaning that every book sent down from Allah has a specific time in which it is revealed and acted upon (the Torah having a time, the Gospel a time, and the Quran a time)—is a view not worth considering.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ** — **Allah effaces or confirms whatever He wishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book.** (13:39). To efface (المحو) something is to remove its trace and mark. It is said: "I effaced the writing" when you remove the lines and marks within it. The Exalted said: **وَيَمْحُ اللهُ الْباطِلَ وَيُحِقُّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ** — **...and Allah will efface falsehood and confirm the truth by His words.** (42:24), meaning He removes the traces of falsehood, just as He said: **فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفاءً** — **...As for the scum, it leaves as dross...** (13:17). And He said: **وَجَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ آيَتَيْنِ فَمَحَوْنا آيَةَ اللَّيْلِ وَجَعَلْنا آيَةَ النَّهارِ مُبْصِرَةً** — **We made the night and the day two signs. Then We effaced the sign of the night and We made the sign of the day a source of visibility...** (17:12), meaning We removed the trace of visibility from the night. Thus, effacement (المحو) is close in meaning to abrogation (النسخ); it is said: "The sun abrogated the shadow" when it removes its trace and mark.

Effacement in the verse is contrasted with confirmation (الإثبات), which is to establish a thing in its place such that it does not move or waver. It is said: "I confirmed the peg in the ground" when you fix it such that it does not move or leave its center. Thus, effacement is the removal of a thing after its establishment with its mark, and it is frequently used in the context of writing.

The occurrence of His saying **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ** after His saying **لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتابٌ**, and its connection to it on one side and to His saying **وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ** on the other, makes it evident that the intended meaning is the effacement and confirmation of books within their times and terms. Thus, the book that Allah confirmed in the first term, He may efface in the second term if He wills, and confirm another book; so a book continues to be effaced and another confirmed.

If we consider what is in the Book as a sign (آية), and everything is a sign, it is correct to say that He continues to efface a sign and confirm a sign, as indicated by His saying: **ما نَنْسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنْسِها نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِنْها أَوْ مِثْلِها** — **For any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring another which is better than it, or one similar to it.** (2:106), and His saying: **وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ** — **When We change a verse in place of another verse...** (16:101).

Thus, His saying **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ**, despite its generality, serves as a justification (التعليل) for His saying **لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتابٌ**. The meaning is that every time has a book specific to it, so they differ. The variation of books according to the variation of times and terms only appears from the side of Divine disposal through His will, not from the side of their own variation in themselves—as if a book were inherently determined for each term such that it could not change. Rather, Allah, the Glorified, is the One who determines that by substituting one book for another, effacing one and confirming another.

His saying: **وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ** — **...and with Him is the Mother of the Book** (13:39), meaning its source (أصله), for the "mother" (الأم) is the source from which a thing originates and to which it returns. This is a rebuttal of a potential objection and an explanation of the reality of the matter. For the variation in the state of the book written for a term—through effacement and confirmation, i.e., the changing of the written ruling and the decreed statement from time to time—might suggest that matters and decrees do not have a fixed form with Allah, the Glorified. It might suggest that His ruling merely follows external causes and factors that necessitate it, like our own rulings and decrees as conscious creatures. Or it might suggest that His ruling is haphazard (جزافي), having no inherent determination, and that nothing external influences its determination—as simple-minded people might imagine that one who possesses absolute ownership (مِلك) and absolute authority (سلطنة) may intend whatever he wills and do whatever he wants with absolute freedom, without observing any constraint, condition, or system. Thus, no fixed form for any of His acts or decrees would exist with Him. Yet the Exalted has said: **ما يُبَدَّلُ الْقَوْلُ لَدَيَّ** — **The word is not altered with Me...** (50:29), and He said: **وَكُلُّ شَيْءٍ عِنْدَهُ بِمِقْدارٍ** — **...and everything is with Him by measure.** (13:8), among other verses.

So He rebutted this objection with His saying: **وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ**, meaning the source of the genus of the Book and the fixed command to which these books—which are effaced and confirmed according to times and terms—return. If the Mother of the Book itself accepted effacement and confirmation, it would be like them, not their source. And if there were no source for it, then effacement and confirmation in the acts of the Exalted would either be dependent on external matters that necessitate it—in which case He, the Exalted, would be compelled and overcome by external factors and causes like us, whereas "Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment"—or it would be dependent on nothing at all, which is haphazardness (الجزاف) that would disrupt the order of creation and the single general governance that links things together. His presence is far above such a thing. The Exalted said: **وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ ما خَلَقْناهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ** — **We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with the truth...** (44:38-39).

In summary, the content of the verse is that for Allah, the Glorified, in every time and term, there is a writing (كتاب)—that is, a ruling and a decree—and that He effaces what He wills of these books, rulings, and decrees, and confirms what He wills. That is, He changes the decree fixed for one time and places in the second time another decree in its stead. However, with Him, in relation to every time, there is a decree that does not change and does not accept effacement or confirmation; it is the source (الأصل) to which the other decrees return and from which they originate, and He effaces and confirms according to what it necessitates.

From the verse, several points become clear:

First: The law of effacement and confirmation is general for all events that are subject to terms and times, which includes everything in the heavens and the earth and what is between them. The Exalted said: **ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** — **We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with the truth and for a stated term.** (46:3).

This is due to the generality of His saying: **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ**. The fact that the specific context (المورد) concerns the signs of Prophethood does not necessitate the restriction of the verse, for the context does not restrict the general application.

By this, the invalidity of several views becomes apparent: (1) that this refers only to legal rulings, which is abrogation (النسخ); (2) that it refers to permissible acts recorded in the scrolls of deeds, which Allah effaces and replaces with obedience or disobedience for which there is recompense; (3) that it is the effacing of the sins of believers out of grace and the confirmation of sins for disbelievers as punishment; (4) that it is in matters where supplication (الدعاء) and charity (الصدقة) have an effect, such as trials, calamities, and narrowness of livelihood; (5) that effacement is the removal of sins through repentance (التوبة) and confirmation is the changing of evil deeds into good deeds; (6) that it is the effacing of whatever generations Allah wills and the confirmation of other generations after them; (7) that it is the effacing of the moon and the confirmation of the sun, which is the effacing of the sign of the night and making the sign of the day a source of visibility; (8) that it is the effacing of this world and the confirmation of the Hereafter; (9) that it is in the souls during sleep, when Allah takes them and then sends back those He wills and withholds those He wills; (10) that it is in the lifespans (الآجال) written on the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر), where Allah effaces what He wills of them and confirms what He wills.

These and similar views are opinions for which there is absolutely no evidence in the wording to restrict the noble verse. The verse possesses an undeniable generality, and necessary observation (المشاهدة الضرورية) accords with it. For the law of change is operative in all parts of the manifest world; there is nothing that, when compared across two points in time in its existence, does not show change in its essence, attributes, and actions. At the same time, if these things are considered in themselves and according to their occurrence, they are found to be fixed and unchanging, for a thing does not change from the way it has occurred.

Thus, manifest things have two aspects: an aspect of change that entails death and life, cessation and subsistence, and types of transformation and substitution; and an aspect of fixity that does not change from what it is. These are either the Book of Effacement and Confirmation and the Mother of the Book themselves, or two matters resulting from the two Books. In any case, the verse is applicable to both these aspects.

Second: For Allah, the Glorified, there is in everything a fixed decree (قضاء ثابت) that does not change. By this, the invalidity of the view mentioned by some—that every decree accepts change—becomes apparent. They argued for this using scattered narrations and supplications indicating that, as well as verses and reports stating that supplication and charity repel an evil decree. The response is that such change occurs in the non-finalized decree (القضاء غير المحتوم).

Third: Decree (القضاء) is divided into changeable and unchangeable. The remainder of the discussion on this verse will be completed shortly, Allah willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنْ ما نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ الَّذِي نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِنَّما عَلَيْكَ الْبَلاغُ وَعَلَيْنَا الْحِسابُ** — **Whether We show you part of what We promise them, or take you away, your duty is only to deliver [the message], and with Us is the reckoning.** (13:40). **إِنْ ما** consists of the conditional *in* (إن) and the extra *ma* (ما) for emphasis, the evidence for which is the entry of the emphatic *nun* (نون التأكيد) into the verb following it.

The verse clarifies the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله function, which is to occupy himself solely with the matter of warning and delivery (التبليغ). He should not follow their desires regarding the descent of a sign upon him as they proposed, to the extent that he should not even wait for the result of his delivery or the arrival of the punishment Allah promised them.

The verse also indicates that Divine reckoning (الحساب الإلهي) occurs in this world just as it occurs in the Hereafter.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها** — **Have they not seen that We visit the land, reducing it from its edges?** (13:41) etc. This is a discourse intended for taking heed (العبرة), presented after the threat of destruction was given to them. From this, it is known that "visiting the land and reducing it from its edges" is a metaphor for the reduction of its inhabitants through death and destruction. Thus, the verse is similar to His saying: **بَلْ مَتَّعْنا هؤُلاءِ وَآباءَهُمْ حَتَّى طالَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْعُمُرُ أَفلا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها أَفَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ** — **Rather We enjoyed these and their fathers until old age was far advanced for them. Do they not see how We visit the land, reducing it from its edges? Is it they who will be the victors?** (21:44).

The view of some—that the meaning is: "Have the people of Mecca not seen that We visit their land and reduce it from its edges by the Muslims conquering the villages one by one, so they should fear that We will conquer their city and take vengeance upon them"—is refuted by the fact that the Surah is Meccan, and those conquests only occurred after the Migration. Furthermore, the verses, with their threat, look toward their destruction in the Battle of Badr and elsewhere, not toward the conquest of Mecca.

His saying: **وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ وَهُوَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ** — **...Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment, and He is swift at reckoning.** (13:41). He intends by this that victory belongs to Allah, the Glorified, for He judges, and there is no one against His judgment to reverse it so as to overcome Him by prevention or rejection. He, the Glorified, reckons every deed as soon as it occurs without delay, such that no one else can interfere with it. Thus, His saying **وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ** etc., carries the same meaning as His saying at the end of the verse from Surah al-Anbiya mentioned above: **أَفَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ** — **...Is it they who will be the victors?** (21:44).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقَدْ مَكَرَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَلِلَّهِ الْمَكْرُ جَمِيعاً** — **Those who were before them [also] plotted; but all plotting belongs to Allah...** (13:42) until the end of the verse. That is: those before them also plotted, but their plotting did not benefit them, and they were unable to prevent Us from visiting the land and reducing it from its edges. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, owns all plotting; He nullifies it and returns it upon its perpetrators. So let them take heed.

His saying: **يَعْلَمُ ما تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ** — **...He knows what every soul earns...** (13:42) serves as a justification for His ownership of all plotting. For plotting only succeeds when the one plotted against is ignorant of it; but if He knows it, then its nullification is with Him.

His saying: **وَسَيَعْلَمُ الْكُفَّارُ لِمَنْ عُقْبَى الدَّارِ** — **...and the faithless will soon know for whom is the ultimate abode.** (13:42). This is a termination of the argument by asserting that the matter of things reaching their ultimate ends is among the necessary, objective realities that will not fail to occur. They will witness it with their own eyes, so there is no need for lengthy or elaborate notification; they will soon know.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is narrated that Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim reported from Mujahid who said: "When the verse **وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** (and an apostle may not bring a sign except by Allah’s leave) was revealed, the Quraysh said: 'We do not see you, O Muhammad, possessing anything, and the matter has already been concluded.' So this verse was revealed as a threat and a warning to them: **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ** (Allah effaces or confirms whatever He wishes)—meaning if We wish, We can bring about from Our command whatever We wish."

**I say:** The verse, as previously explained, is foreign to this meaning. At the end of this hadith, it says: "And Allah brings about in every Ramadan, so Allah effaces what He wills and confirms of the people's provisions, their calamities, what He gives them, and what He apportions for them." In another narration from Jabir from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding the verse, he said: "He effaces from the provision and increases in it, and He effaces from the term (الأجل) and increases in it." This is by way of giving examples, while the verse is more general.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was asked about His saying: **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ**. He said: "That is every Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر); He raises and lowers and provides—except for life, death, wretchedness (الشقاوة), and happiness (السعادة), for those do not change."

**I say:** This narration, besides contradicting the very numerous narrations reported from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام, and the companions, also contradicts the generality of the verse and the proof of reason. Similar to it is what is narrated from Ibn Umar from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Allah effaces what He wills and confirms—except for wretchedness, happiness, life, and death."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh and Ibn Asakir narrated from Ali that he asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about this verse, and he said to him: "I shall certainly delight your eye with its interpretation, and I shall certainly delight the eye of my community after me with its interpretation: Charity (الصدقة) given correctly, kindness to parents, and performing good deeds transform wretchedness into happiness, increase the lifespan, and protect against evil ends."

**I say:** This narration does no more than mention some of the instances (مصاديق) of the verse.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Hisham ibn Salim, Hafs ibn al-Bakhtari, and others, from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام regarding this verse: **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ**. He said: "And is anything effaced except what was confirmed? And is anything confirmed except what was not?"

**I say:** al-Ayyashi also narrated it in his *Tafsir* from Jamil from him (عليه السلام).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Fudayl ibn Yasar who said: "I heard Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام saying: 'Among matters are those that are finalized (محتومة), which will inevitably occur. And among matters are those that are suspended (موقوفة) with Allah; He brings forward in them what He wills, and He effaces what He wills and confirms of them what He wills. He has not informed anyone of those—meaning the suspended ones. As for what the messengers brought, they will occur; He does not prove Himself, His prophet, or His angels wrong.'"

**I say:** It is narrated through another chain, and similarly in *al-Kafi* with its chain from Fudayl from him عليه‌السلام with the same meaning.

In the same source, from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام who said: "Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام used to say: 'Were it not for a verse in the Book of Allah, I would have told you of what has been and what will be until the Day of Resurrection.' I said to him: 'Which verse?' He said: 'Allah said: **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ**.'"

**I say:** Its meaning is that what appears from the verse is that Allah, the Glorified, only wants His creation to live in ignorance of future events so that they may fulfill the duties of their lives guided by ordinary causes and driven by fear and hope. The full manifestation of future events would spoil this Divine purpose. Thus, it is the reason for refraining from speaking [of the future], not the fear that Allah would prove him wrong through *al-bada'* (البداء) — *the manifestation of a Divine decree contrary to what was previously apparent* — for he is secure from that. Thus, there is no contradiction between this narration and the one before it.

In the same source, from Fudayl ibn Yasar from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام who said: "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted, wrote a book in which is what was and what is to be. He placed it before Him; so what He willed of it He brought forward, and what He willed He delayed, and what He willed He effaced, and what He willed occurred, and what He did not will did not occur."

In the same source, from Ibn Sinan from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام who said: "Indeed, Allah brings forward what He wills and delays what He wills, and effaces what He wills and confirms what He wills, and with Him is the Mother of the Book." And he said: "Every matter that Allah intends is in His knowledge before He establishes it. Nothing appears (يبدو) to Him except that it was already in His knowledge; indeed, nothing appears to Allah out of ignorance."

**I say:** The narrations regarding *al-bada'* from them عليه‌السلام are numerous and widely transmitted (مستفيضة), so no weight is given to what has been reported from some that it is a solitary report (خبر واحد).

The narration, as you see, negates *al-bada'* in the sense of His, the Exalted, gaining knowledge of something He was previously ignorant of—meaning a change in His knowledge in His essence, as might happen with us; Allah is far exalted above that. Rather, it is the manifestation of a command from Him, the Exalted, a second time after what was manifest from Him was contrary to it the first time. Thus, it is the effacing of the first and the confirmation of the second, while Allah, the Glorified, is knowledgeable of both.

This is something that no person of intellect can deny. For matters and events have an existence according to what their deficient causes—whether a cause, a condition, or an obstacle—necessitate, which might fail to occur. And they have an existence according to what their complete causes and reasons necessitate, which is fixed, not suspended, and does not fail to occur. The two Books—I mean the Book of Effacement and Confirmation and the Mother of the Book—are either two matters followed by these two stages of the existence of things (one of which accepts effacement and confirmation and the other accepts only fixity), or they are those two stages themselves. In any case, the manifestation of a command or a will from Him, the Exalted, after what was manifest was contrary to it, is clear and should not be doubted.

What I believe is that the dispute over the affirmation of *al-bada'*, as appears from the traditions of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), and its negation, as appears from others, is a verbal dispute (نزاع لفظي). For this reason, we have not dedicated a separate section to this discussion as is the custom of authors. One evidence that the dispute is verbal is their arguing for the negation of *al-bada'* from Him, the Exalted, on the grounds that it entails a change in His knowledge—whereas that is a necessary consequence of *al-bada'* in the sense it is interpreted regarding us, not *al-bada'* in the sense it is interpreted in the reports regarding Him, the Exalted.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Hakim narrated from Abu al-Darda' that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ** with the lightened form (مخففة).

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said regarding His saying **نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها** (reducing it from its edges): 'The passing away of the scholars.'"

In *al-Majma'*, from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام: "We reduce it by the passing away of its scholars, its jurists, and its best people."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Ali from the one he mentioned from Jabir from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام who said: "Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام used to say: 'What makes my soul generous regarding the speed of death or killing among us is the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: **أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها** (Have they not seen that We visit the land, reducing it from its edges?).' He said: 'The loss of the scholars.'"

**I say:** It is as if the meaning is that it makes my soul generous that Allah, the Exalted, attributed the taking of the scholars to Himself and not to anyone else, so death or killing becomes pleasant for me.
